coredata.dc:title: Capacity estimation of retired lithium-ion batteries using random charging segments from massive real-world data
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Pengfei
Liang, Junli
Liu, Yunlong
Wu, Ji
Song, Qi
Li, Xiangyang
coredata.dc:description: The repurposing of retired lithium-ion batteries from electric vehicles is a critical strategy for reducing carbon emissions. Capacity estimation plays a key role in facilitating this process. However, achieving an accurate estimation remains challenging due to the inherent variability of batteries and the complexity of real-world conditions. Existing methods often rely on small datasets and feature extraction, which limits their generalizability and leads to high testing costs. To overcome these limitations, we compile the two largest known datasets of retired lithium-ion batteries and develop a tailored neural network model capable of directly capturing both long-term and short-term temporal patterns. By utilizing randomly segmented charging curves as input, the proposed model reduces the cost of charging-discharging tests in practical applications. The experimental results demonstrate that the model achieves satisfactory performance in capacity estimation, supporting the optimization of resource use and reduction of environmental pollution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: capacity estimation
charging segments
deep learning
retired batteries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research was partially supported by \u201CPioneer\u201D and \u201CLeading Goose\u201D R&D Program of Zhejiang 2023C01029 , the China National Natural Science Foundation with no. 62132018 , and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under grant JZ2023YQTD0073 . The data used in this research were provided by Anhui Guoqi Technology Co., Ltd. The authors thank all members of the lab for their support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Properties of two datasets collected from real-world conditions
(A) QV curves of charging stage on AOC23.
(B) Capacity distribution histogram of complete curves on AOC23.
(C) Capacity distribution histogram of segments on AOC23.
(D) QV curves of charging stage on AOC15.
(E) Capacity distribution histogram of complete curves on AOC15.
(F) Capacity distribution histogram of segments on AOC15.
Performance of different models at an r of 0.6 on AOC23
All six models successfully capture the mapping from segments to corresponding capacities.
(A) MLP.
(B) LSTM.
(C) TCN.
(D) 1D-CNN.
(E) GAF-CNN.
(F) 2D-CNN.
Performance of different models at an r of 0.6 on AOC15
Only TCN, GAF-CNN, and 2D-CNN can extract appropriate patterns from segments. The error of the 2D-CNN is lower than those of the other two models on most of the segments.
(A) MLP.
(B) LSTM.
(C) TCN.
(D) 1D-CNN.
(E) GAF-CNN.
(F) 2D-CNN.
RMSEs, MAEs, and MAPEs of capacity estimation results on AOC23 and AOC15 with respect to different segment lengths
2D-CNN consistently performs best on all three metrics (except when r is 0.4, where RMSE is slightly higher than GAF-CNN). Data are the best from five independent experiments.
(A–C) Capacity estimation results on AOC23.
(D–F) Capacity estimation results on AOC15.

Effect of
h / w
Data are the best from five independent experiments.
(A) RMSEs, MAEs, and MAPEs on AOC23.
(B) RMSEs, MAEs, and MAPEs on AOC15.
Comparison among differing CNN backbone types and scales
The size of the scatter points represents the number of model parameters.
(A) Results on AOC23.
(B) Results on AOC15.
Channel importance heatmaps for the retired batteries
Darker regions represent more important channels.

(A)
SoH ≥ 0.8
on AOC23.

(B)
SoH < 0.8
on AOC23.

(C)
SoH ≥ 0.8
on AOC15.

(D)
SoH < 0.8
on AOC15.
The overall pipeline for LiB capacity estimation using the proposed 2D-CNN
First, data were collected and preprocessed. Then, we refined the 2D-CNN in an offline training phase. Finally, the performance was evaluated using the test data in an online estimation phase.
Details of the 2D-CNN
First, 1D time-series data are transformed into 2D image data. After that, ResNet-10 is adopted as the CNN backbone to establish the mapping relationship between the input and the output.
(A) The overall pipeline.
(B) Details of ResNet-10.
Performance of different models at an r of 0.6

head.ce:title: Capacity estimation of retired lithium-ion batteries using random charging segments from massive real-world data

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Computer Science and Technology, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Computer Science and Technology
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Computer Science and Technology, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Automotive and Transportation Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, Anhui 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Automotive and Transportation Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Automotive and Transportation Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, Anhui 230009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The repurposing of retired lithium-ion batteries from electric vehicles is a critical strategy for reducing carbon emissions. Capacity estimation plays a key role in facilitating this process. However, achieving an accurate estimation remains challenging due to the inherent variability of batteries and the complexity of real-world conditions. Existing methods often rely on small datasets and feature extraction, which limits their generalizability and leads to high testing costs. To overcome these limitations, we compile the two largest known datasets of retired lithium-ion batteries and develop a tailored neural network model capable of directly capturing both long-term and short-term temporal patterns. By utilizing randomly segmented charging curves as input, the proposed model reduces the cost of charging-discharging tests in practical applications. The experimental results demonstrate that the model achieves satisfactory performance in capacity estimation, supporting the optimization of resource use and reduction of environmental pollution.

Capacity estimation for lithium-ion batteries is a key aspect for potentially repurposing retired electric vehicle batteries. Here, Zhou et al. use real-world data from retired lithium-ion batteries and develop a neural network for capacity estimation with reduced need for charge-discharge testing.

ce:section.ce:para:
Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) are widely used in electric vehicles owing to their high energy density, low self-discharge rate, and lack of memory effects. 1
,
2
,
3 In recent years, the growing popularity of electric vehicles has significantly increased the demand for LiBs while also raising the need for efficient repurposing of retired batteries. 4 According to the estimation provided by the International Energy Agency, one-third of the vehicles on China’s roads will be electrified by 2030, with the United States and the European Union approaching one-fifth. 5 Concurrently, global battery recycling capacity is projected to exceed 1,500 GWh, with 70% of this capacity being located in China. The battery law enacted by the European Union in July 2023, 6 and revised in July 2024, mandates that battery manufacturers implement efficient recycling and repurposing systems to ensure the environmentally responsible disposal of retired batteries. Therefore, developing advanced post-processing technologies for retired LiBs is crucial for enhancing resource efficiency, promoting carbon neutrality, and reducing environmental pollution, making it a key focus of current research. 7
,
8
,
9

The battery capacity, defined as the amount of electricity a battery cell can release during a full discharging cycle, is a key indicator of the condition of retired batteries. 10
,
11 Over time, battery capacity declines due to factors such as lithium-ion inventory loss, electrolyte consumption, and internal resistance increase. 12 This decline is influenced by several factors, including the inherent characteristics of the batteries in the factory and their operating environments. 13 Electric vehicle LiBs, which typically consist of multiple cells, are often retired as battery packs. The health state of the individual cells within a pack can vary significantly, with some cells in good condition and others significantly aged, 14 so it is essential to obtain the capacity of each cell separately to facilitate their optimal repurposing and promote resource recycling. In practical production and operation environments, the capacity of a cell is typically determined through complete charging and discharging experiments, which are both time consuming and labor intensive. Consequently, there has been significant interest from both industry and academia in developing efficient capacity estimation methods for retired LiBs. These methods can be broadly categorized into two types: model driven and data driven. 15

Model-driven methods mainly include mechanism models such as equivalent circuit models and electrochemical models. Wang et al. extracted four health indicators (HIs) related to capacity based on the electrochemical mechanisms of retired LiBs and evaluated the matching of internal resistance and the remaining capacity through a strategy of cross-validating the resistance, remaining capacity, and state of health (SoH). 16 Another approach examines the aging of the mechanism model parameters to establish HIs that characterize the capacity loss mechanism. 17 Additionally, Ahmeid et al. used an open-circuit voltage to create an equivalent circuit model that only required terminal voltage and discharging current as inputs, significantly reducing the test time. 18 These model-driven methods typically offer good inherent interpretability, but building these models requires a deep understanding of the battery’s internal chemical processes and involves complex and time-consuming validation tests. Furthermore, different battery types have varying chemical mechanisms, which often results in poor model transferability, posing challenges for model-driven methods amid the rapid development of battery technology.

Simultaneously, advancements in deep learning have propelled data-driven methods to gain prominence among researchers. Some studies have used indicators extracted from charging and discharging experiments to estimate the capacity of retired batteries. For instance, Braco et al. proposed a fast capacity estimation method using HIs, such as the initial open-circuit voltage, alternating current (AC) internal resistance, and direct current (DC) resistance, reducing the application costs. 19 Furthermore, an improved support vector regression method was introduced to integrate three HIs related to the capacity loss mechanism. 20 The accuracy of HI-based methods is usually highly dependent on the choice of indicators. Since different HIs often need to be designed for different batteries, their practical applications are limited. To address this issue, many studies directly use charging and discharging curves as input. Some assume access to complete charging-discharging data. Lin et al. converted the retired battery charging curve into an image using the Gramian angular field (GAF) and Markov transition field techniques and introduced ConvNeXt 21 and Swin Transformer 22 networks to screen retired batteries based on their capacity. 23
,
24 However, methods based on complete charging-discharging data are costly in practical applications. For large-capacity batteries in electric vehicles, a full charging-discharging cycle can take several hours or even longer. Moreover, longer test cycles impose greater demands on the robustness of the testing system. As a result, capacity estimation methods based on segmented curves have gained prominence in recent years. Liu et al. used a residual convolutional neural network (CNN) to accurately estimate battery capacity using only small segments of the original measurement data. 25 However, the use of segments of large-scale retired batteries as direct input remains unexplored.

Our study aimed to accurately estimate the capacity of retired batteries using randomly segmented charging data in practical settings. In order to address the three main challenges to estimating the capacity of retired LiBs, which are the limited sample size in experiments, reliance on feature selection, and the high application cost of using complete curves, we collected two of the largest known datasets of retired batteries and proposed a two-dimensional (2D) CNN (2D-CNN) model. This model uses randomly segmented charging curves as input and directly extracts long-term trends and short-term fluctuations from original time-series data. The experimental results indicate that, using the 60% charging segment as an example, the model achieved root-mean-square errors (RMSEs) of 0.679 Ah for batteries with a rated capacity of 23 Ah and 0.206 Ah for batteries with a rated capacity of 15.5 Ah. Moreover, compared to methods using complete charging-discharging curves, this approach reduces the test time by more than 65% and energy consumption by nearly 9%. These findings demonstrate that the proposed 2D-CNN method can reduce testing costs while maintaining high accuracy, providing a cheap solution for retired LiB capacity estimation, thereby promoting effective echelon repurposing of retired LiBs.

The contributions of this work are as follows.
(1)
Two sets of retired LiB charging-discharging data were collected from real-world operating conditions. To the best of our knowledge, these are the two largest datasets in relevant published research, containing 2,276 and 2,354 battery cells, respectively. A larger number of batteries provides a more representative distribution of real-world batteries.
(2)
Randomly segmented charging data were used as the input. Due to variations in the initial capacity of each battery cell at the start of testing, methods based on specific intervals of charging-discharging curves introduce additional experimental overhead. The approach of using random segments satisfies the practical demands of real-world scenarios.
(3)
A carefully designed deep learning architecture is proposed. By explicitly considering both long-term and short-term patterns directly from time-series data, the model outperforms commonly used deep learning models across multiple metrics, demonstrating its effectiveness.

In practice, each battery cell exhibits slight variations in its physical and chemical properties upon leaving the factory. Furthermore, these batteries experience distinct aging processes owing to their different usage environments and habits. Therefore, in addition to capacity, the charging-discharging curve of a cell is also related to other factors. 26
,
27 This complexity presents a significant challenge in accurately estimating the capacity of retired LiBs using only random charging segments.

In commercial applications, retired batteries from electric vehicles are initially disassembled into individual cells, followed by screening and testing for potential echelon utilization. The retired batteries employed in this study consist of two types of lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries from Gotion Hi-Tech, specifically the IFP2265146-23Ah and IFR32135-15.5Ah models. The IFP2265146-23Ah is made up of square cells with a nominal capacity of 23 Ah and a maximum charging voltage of 3.65 V, while the IFR32135-15.5 Ah is made of cylindrical cells with a nominal capacity of 15.5 Ah and a maximum charging voltage of 3.9 V. The complete nominal specifications are detailed in Table S1. The test procedure for determining the charging-discharging curves of the retired LiBs used in this study is as follows.
Step 0: prior to testing, each battery cell was fully discharged to a 0% state of charge (SoC) level.
Step 1: the cell was charged at a constant rate of 1 C from a 0% SoC level until a cutoff voltage of 3.65 V was reached.
Step 2: the cell continued to charge in the constant voltage stage at 3.65 V until a cutoff current of C/50 was reached.
Step 3: The fully charged cell then rested without being charged or discharged. This relaxation period stabilizes the internal dynamics of the electrochemical mechanism and minimizes data acquisition differences.
Step 4: During the discharging phase, each cell was discharged at a constant current until the voltage reached a cutoff threshold of 2.5 V.

Voltage and other feasible indicators are sampled every 3 s during the testing process. Following this process, two datasets, AOC23 and AOC15, were generated by preprocessing, consisting of 2,276 IFP2265146-23Ah battery cells and 2,354 IFR32135-15.5Ah battery cells, respectively. To our knowledge, these represent the two largest datasets of retired LiBs. The basic information of these datasets is summarized in Table S2. In the AOC23 dataset, the recorded capacities range from 12.51 to 23.98 Ah, with an average capacity of 18.51 Ah and an average charging timestamp of 2,562 (about 128 min). Similarly, the AOC15 dataset exhibits capacities ranging from 8.31 to 12.50 Ah, with an average capacity of 11.48 Ah and an average charging timestamp of 1,392 (approximately 69 min).

Unlike battery aging datasets such as CALCE, 28 Oxford, 29 and others, 30
,
31
,
32
,
33 which contain multiple cycle charging-discharging curves of a small number of battery cells, our retired datasets consist of single charging-discharging curves from a large number of cells. Additionally, the batteries in our study originate from actual working conditions, having undergone several incomplete charging-discharging processes under varying temperatures, humidity levels, and usage habits. As illustrated in Figures 1A and 1D, the QV (capacity-voltage) curves during the charging stage are complex. This complexity is evident in that, although the QV curves demonstrate an overall trend corresponding to capacity (as indicated by the green arrow, which traces the progression from the low-capacity cells in the orange frame to the high-capacity cells in the blue frame), there remain significant non-trend components that cannot be overlooked (in the black frame). In contrast, the QV curves for the aging dataset exhibit a more pronounced trend with a limited presence of non-trend components, as illustrated in Figure S1. Moreover, the IC (incremental capacity) curves and DV (differential voltage) curves of the charging process also show that the curves are highly intricate in real-world testing, as shown in Figure S2.

Our datasets offer a realistic representation of retired LiB behavior in practical applications, making capacity estimation greatly challenging yet highly relevant to real-world scenarios. The variability in our data highlights the necessity of developing robust models capable of handling diverse and incomplete charging-discharging data, thereby enhancing the generalizability and accuracy of capacity estimation methods for retired batteries.

In addition to the complexity of the curves, the distribution of battery capacity is significantly imbalanced, as depicted in Figures 1B and 1E. The AOC23 dataset exhibits a bimodal distribution with two distinct peaks: one around 15.4 Ah and another between 20.55 and 21.70 Ah, with the latter being more pronounced. The imbalance is even more obvious in the AOC15 dataset, where over 75% of the data fall between 11.2 and 12.1 Ah. This skewed distribution may result in the underrepresentation of certain capacity intervals during model evaluation, potentially leading to a distorted assessment of model performance. To mitigate this, resampling was implemented, as detailed in the methods section, to ensure a more representative and fair evaluation of the model’s capabilities. The distribution of the processed data is depicted in Figures 1C and 1F.

The dataset, comprising the original complete charging curves, was first divided into three subsets in an 8:1:1 ratio. Segmented charging curves were then randomly sampled from each subset to form the training, validation, and test sets (as detailed in the methods section). To ensure uniformity, the constant padding method was applied, standardizing all segments to the same length. For every segment in each dataset, the accumulated capacity and voltage are adopted as the input of the model.

The initial capacity of a retired cell is often random in real-world production environments. To simulate this, the accumulated capacity (
Δ Q
), rather than the absolute capacity, is used as one of the model inputs. The accumulated capacity quantifies the charged capacity during a partial charging process. Specifically, the accumulated capacity at the
i th
timestamp of a segment is expressed as
Δ
Q i
=
Q i
−
Q
i n i t
, where
Q i
represents the absolute capacity at the
i th
timestamp and
Q
i n i t
=
Q 1
denotes the residual capacity of the battery cell at the onset of simulated partial charging. Although these two parameters cannot be directly measured, their difference,
Δ
Q i
, can be obtained through coulomb counting. Another input, voltage (V), is the electric potential difference across the battery terminals. The voltage at the
i th
timestamp of a segment is then denoted as
V i
. These two factors,
Δ Q
and V, are crucial inputs for capacity estimation models, as variations in
Δ Q
and V significantly impact the estimation of battery capacity. Tracking these parameters allows for an accurate estimation of the battery capacity. Consequently, the 1D time-series data, i.e., segmented charging data from a random SoC level, are then named
X
1 D
=
V
Δ Q
∈
ℝ
r · L
× 2
, represented as follows:
(Equation 1)
X
1 D
=
V
Δ Q
=
V 1
Δ
Q 1
V 2
Δ
Q 2
⋮
⋮
V
r · L
Δ
Q
r · L
r · L
× 2
where L represents the average number of timestamps across all complete charging curves and r denotes the ratio of the segment length to the complete charging curve length. Following imbalanced sampling, data normalization was performed to enhance model performance, resulting in the normalized
X
1 D
'
, which is the input of the proposed network. Additionally, Gaussian noise-based data augmentation was employed to regularize the model during training.

Considering the complex long-term trends and short-term fluctuations in segmented charging data, we designed a neural network architecture, termed 2D-CNN, which effectively captures both types of patterns. Assuming that the image input to the CNN backbone is
X
2 D
∈
ℝ
h × w × 2
, the method of converting 1D time-series data into 2D image data is shown as
(Equation 2)
X
2 D
=
Reshape
h × w
AdaptiveAvgPool
h · w
X
1 D
'
where
AdaptiveAvgPool
h · w
adaptively performs upsampling or downsampling on the 1D time-series data along the temporal dimension, making it compatible with
Reshape
h × w
. h and w represent the numbers of rows and columns of the transformed 2D images, respectively. This transformation introduces two types of localities to the transformed 2D images: localities among adjacent timestamps (rows, representing short-term fluctuation patterns) and adjacent time intervals (columns, representing long-term trend patterns). Consequently, the temporal 2D variations can be efficiently processed by 2D convolutional kernels without modifications to deep learning operators. We used a CNN backbone to map
X
2 D
to capacity, thus providing an effective reference for the battery’s echelon utilization.

For comparative analysis, five existing neural network models were employed to address the capacity estimation problem using randomly segmented data as input. These models include the multilayer perceptron (MLP), 34
,
35 long short-term memory (LSTM), 36 temporal convolutional network (TCN), 37 1D-CNN, 38 and GAF-CNN, 39 with a detailed introduction in Note S1. Except for the 1D-CNN, which followed the architecture of the original paper, the scales (i.e., the number of parameters) of the other models were kept comparable to those of the 2D-CNN. All models were implemented in Python using the PyTorch framework and evaluated on the same training, validation, and test sets to ensure consistency in assessing performance. Each model received
X
1 D
'
as the input and produced capacity as the output.

Taking an r of 0.6 as an example, where the segment length is 60% of the average full charging length, Figures 2 and 3 display the capacity estimation results for the two battery datasets. In the AOC23 dataset, all six models effectively learn the relationship between the input (random charging segments) and the output (capacity) across the entire range. Among these, the two CNN models based on 2D convolution exhibit low errors for most data points, with histogram counts exceeding 600 on the vertical axis. Of the two, the 2D-CNN performs slightly better than the GAF-CNN. On the AOC15 dataset, however, only TCN, GAF-CNN, and 2D-CNN achieve consistent estimation accuracy across the full interval. The MLP struggles with capturing features on the curve due to its lack of explicit time-series modeling. The LSTM model shows significant errors, likely because of long-term dependency issues. The 1D-CNN, constrained by its small model size, fails to deliver accurate estimations. Overall, 2D-CNN outperforms TCN, GAF-CNN, and others, especially on samples that are more challenging to estimate, demonstrating the best overall performance.

The error metrics used in this study include RMSE, mean absolute error (MAE), and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), as detailed in Note S2. The 2D-CNN model outperforms all others across these three metrics on both test sets when r is 0.6. The consistent results across the two datasets indicate that the observation is not coincidental. For the AOC23 dataset, the RMSE is 0.679 Ah, with an MAE of 0.439 Ah and a MAPE of 2.47%. Similarly, for the AOC15 dataset, the RMSE is 0.206 Ah, with an MAE of 0.152 Ah and a MAPE of 1.48%. Table 1 summarizes the performance metrics of each model when r is 0.6.

To further explore the effect of random segment length on capacity estimation, datasets with r values ranging from 0.4 to 1.0 were generated. Figure 4 presents the RMSE, MAE, and MAPE for the six methods across the two datasets at different segment lengths. Among all models, the proposed 2D-CNN achieves the lowest RMSE, MAE, and MAPE in the vast majority of cases. Generally, a relatively large segment length reduces overall error, eliminates outliers, and narrows the error distribution. However, the LSTM model shows instability due to long input segments, while the 1D-CNN’s performance suffers from a limited model representation ability due to its smaller parameter count. For these two models, performance does not necessarily improve with an increase in r. In contrast, the other four methods demonstrate better estimation results as the segment length increases. The complete experimental results can be found in Tables S3 and S4. While a longer segment improves accuracy, it also requires more testing time and resources, so a balance needs to be struck between estimation accuracy and resource consumption depending on the specific scenario.

Compared to industrial approaches that rely on full charging-discharging curves, the 2D-CNN model requires only segmented charging curves as input. This approach significantly reduces both testing time and energy consumption while preserving estimation accuracy. Even when using the full charging curve, 2D-CNN still cuts the time required for discharging test. To quantitatively compare the segmented charging curve approach with traditional full charging-discharging methods, we used
Δ T = average
(
1 −
T
s e g m e n t
/
T
f u l l
)
and
Δ E = average
(
1 −
E
s e g m e n t
/
E
f u l l
)
to roughly estimate the time and energy savings (where T means time and E represents energy). As shown in Table S5, in the AOC23 dataset, when r is 0.6,
Δ T
is 65.92% and
Δ E
is 8.86%, while for an r of 1,
Δ T
is 43.16% and
Δ E
is 1.34%. Similarly, for the AOC15 dataset, when r is 0.6,
Δ T
is 68.39% and
Δ E
is 16.86%, and for an r of 1,
Δ T
is 47.41% and
Δ E
is 6.68%. In practical applications, retired batteries require recharging before being repurposed after capacity estimation. Our method can also reduce the time and energy required for this recharging step. These results demonstrate that the segmented charging curve approach can significantly lower test time and energy consumption while maintaining accurate capacity estimation. This approach promotes production efficiency and reduces environmental impact.

In the experiment, to maintain consistency in the length of data input across different models,
h · w
was maintained as a constant. Thus, the shape of the
X
2 D
image input to the model, represented by
h / w
, was then treated as a hyperparameter. This part explores the impact of various
h / w
ratios on the estimation accuracy of the test set, as illustrated in Figure 5 and Table S6. For the AOC23 dataset, the errors are minimized when
h / w
is 1, while for the AOC15 dataset, the errors are minimized when
h / w
is 0.1. Additionally, in the AOC23 dataset, the errors are significantly lower when
h / w
is 1 compared to other values. However, in the AOC15 dataset, the errors are not much different across a range of
h / w
values from 0.1 to 10. This suggests that the model’s sensitivity to the
h / w
ratio varies between datasets. Overall, the model achieves the maximum accuracy when
h / w
is approximately between 0.1 and 10. A smaller
h / w
ratio results in the timestamps of the transformed 2D data being spaced farther apart alongside the column, allowing the model to capture longer-term patterns. In contrast, a larger
h / w
ratio enables the model to focus on shorter-term patterns. In addition to that, when
h / w
is too small or too large, the model tends to degenerate into a 1D-CNN, resulting in reduced accuracy.

The 2D-CNN architecture comprises two components: the 1D-to-2D transformation and the CNN backend. In the previous section, we examined the impact of various transformation methods (specifically the
h / w
ratio) on estimation results. In this section, we will investigate the influence of different CNN backbones. Various backbones can yield differing estimation accuracies, along with variations in running time and memory consumption. Our objective is to develop a model that minimizes running time and memory usage while maintaining estimation accuracy.

In industrial applications, understanding model interpretability is essential. Models that lack interpretability can raise concerns regarding their reliability. Post hoc interpretability analysis is commonly employed for deep learning models to assess their behavior across different types of data, thereby providing insights into model interpretability. 43
,
44

In the field of retired battery repurposing, it is widely accepted that batteries with a SoH exceeding 80% are considered to be in relatively good condition. 13
,
45 Accordingly, each dataset used in this study was classified into two groups using an SoH threshold of 80% of the rated capacity. For the same dataset, channel importance heatmaps (see Note S3) were generated by visualizing the contributions of different channels in the final convolutional layer. This approach helps illustrate the varying behaviors of the same trained model when applied to two different batches of data. Figure 7 presents the average heatmaps of each group for both datasets at an r of 0.6, where darker regions correspond to channels that contribute more significantly to capacity estimation.

In the AOC23 dataset, the 2D-CNN model predominantly relies on a limited number of channels, whereas in the AOC15 dataset, the model appears to engage a greater number of channels. Additionally, the model demonstrates different areas of focus depending on the battery’s state within the same dataset. For instance, in the AOC23 dataset, batteries with an SoH above 80% show a clearly dominant channel, while for those with an SoH below 80%, the model requires slightly more and significantly different channels to extract relevant features. These findings indicate that the model is capable of distinguishing batteries with varying health conditions, which is essential for accurate capacity estimation and ensuring the safe and reliable repurposing of retired batteries.

The recycling and repurposing of retired LiBs can reduce environmental pollution and improve energy efficiency. However, current technology for estimating the capacity of retired batteries is in its early stages and hindered by a lack of large-scale datasets. To address this issue, we collected two datasets, each containing 2,276 and 2,354 battery cells for a single charging-discharging process. To our knowledge, these are the largest datasets in this research field, effectively characterizing real-world battery conditions.

Considering that real-world charging data are often segmented, we propose a framework for LiB capacity estimation based on random segments of accumulated capacity and voltage. This framework includes a complete data preprocessing, training, and evaluation process. To reasonably evaluate the model’s representational ability, an intuitive imbalanced sampling method is used. We propose a robust 2D-CNN to capture both long-term and short-term patterns, achieving RMSEs of 0.679 and 0.206 Ah in the two datasets, respectively. The estimation results show that the proposed 2D-CNN model outperforms existing methods such as MLP, LSTM, and TCN. Further studies indicate that the model’s estimation ability is influenced by the shape of the 2D input, and the accuracy of capacity estimation can be improved by adjusting the aspect ratio of the 2D input. Interpretability analysis shows that the model does have discriminative capabilities based on the battery condition.

In summary, our work highlights the potential of deep learning in the capacity estimation of retired LiBs. We anticipate that the proposed framework will promote the repurposing of retired batteries, advance carbon neutrality, and reduce environmental pollution.

As discussed in the results section, the substantial variations among battery cells present two critical challenges: complex charging-discharging profiles and imbalanced capacity distribution. This study introduced a sampling method to address the issue of imbalanced capacity distribution. Additionally, the 2D-CNN was designed to capture both long-term and short-term patterns in time-series data, facilitating the effective feature extraction from complex partial charging curves. The comprehensive pipeline of this method is illustrated in Figure 8 .

A crucial aspect of data preprocessing is addressing data imbalance. This was achieved through imbalanced sampling, ensuring that the resulting data distribution is more balanced. Specifically, the original complete curves were first divided into three sets in an 8:1:1 ratio. These three sets were each subjected to imbalanced sampling to prevent data leakage.

For each set, assuming n segments were required, the curves were initially divided into k groups (
G 1
,
G 2
, … ,
G k
) equidistantly based on their corresponding capacities, with
G i
containing
|
G i
|
curves. The number of samples per curve in
G i
can be then calculated as
(Equation 3)
s i
=
n
k ·
|
G i
|

A sliding window was then employed to extract
s i
segments from each curve in
G i
. Specifically, the complete curve in
G i
was divided into
s i
intervals. From each interval, a random time point was selected as the initial partially sampling point, and segments were then sampled based on the predefined sampling window (determined by
r · L
). For segments with an original length shorter than the required length, the constant padding method was applied to extend their tail, ensuring uniform input length for the model. This preprocessing method alleviates the data imbalance problem, ensuring that the errors in the validation set and the test set more accurately reflect the model’s generalization ability.

In the experiment, accumulated capacity (
Δ Q
), rather than absolute capacity, was used as one of the model inputs. Using
Δ Q
and V as inputs, the 1D time-series data are then represented as
X
1 D
∈
ℝ
r · L
× 2
=
V
Δ Q
. Following imbalanced sampling, data normalization was performed to enhance model performance:
(Equation 4)
X
1 D
'
=
X
1 D
− min
X
1 D
max
X
1 D
− min
X
1 D
+ ε
where
min
( · )
and
max
( · )
are the element-wise minimum and maximum operations. Summation, subtraction, and division are element wise as well. The tiny constant
ε =
10
− 5
is to prevent division by zero errors.

Given the complex long-term trends and short-term fluctuations in segmented charging data, we designed the 2D-CNN model, which can effectively capture both types of patterns. The architecture of this model is depicted in Figure 9 .

Firstly, the 1D time-series data
X
1 D
'
was transformed into 2D image data
X
2 D
∈
ℝ
h × w × 2
. Secondly,
X
2 D
was input into ResNet-10 backbone. ResNet addresses the gradient vanishing and exploding problems in deep neural networks during training by introducing a residual mechanism, and it has demonstrated excellent performance on large datasets such as ImageNet. 46 The estimated capacity
Y ˆ
∈ R
is then formulated as below:
(Equation 5)
Y ˆ
= ResNet
X
2 D
Further information and requests for data and code should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Qi Song (qisong09@ustc.edu.cn).
This study did not generate new unique reagents.

ce:list-item.ce:para: All processed data have been deposited on Zenodo 47 at Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.14562266.
All code of the 2D-CNN can be accessed online on GitHub 48 at Github: https://github.com/mumiao2000/capacity_estimation_of_retired_LiBs.
Any additional information required to reanalyze the data reported in this paper is available from the lead contact upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: The research was partially supported by “Pioneer” and “Leading Goose” R&D Program of Zhejiang 2023C01029, the China National Natural Science Foundation with no. 62132018, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under grant JZ2023YQTD0073. The data used in this research were provided by Anhui Guoqi Technology Co., Ltd. The authors thank all members of the lab for their support.

Conceptualization, Q.S., J.W., and X.L.; methodology, P.Z., Q.S., and J.W.; investigation, P.Z., Q.S., and J.W.; data curation, P.Z. and Y.L.; writing – original draft, P.Z.; writing – review & editing, P.Z., J.L., Q.S., and J.W.; visualization, P.Z. and J.L.
The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work, the authors used ChatGPT in order to polish the paper. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.
Supplemental information can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.xcrp.2025.102444.
